coredata.dc:title: A ratiometric fluorescent strategy for alkaline phosphatase activity assay based on g-C3N4/CoOOH nanohybrid via target-triggered competitive redox reaction
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Shi Gang
Han, Lei
Li, Na
Fan, Yu Zhu
Yang, Yu Zhu
Li, Nian Bing
Luo, Hong Qun
coredata.dc:description: The development of ratiometric fluorescent strategy is very significant and challenging in bioanalysis. Herein, graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) nanosheets and cobalt oxyhydroxide (CoOOH) nanoflakes are exploited for ratiometric fluorescence assay of alkaline phosphatase (ALP) activity. In the g-C3N4/CoOOH nanohybrid, g-C3N4 nanosheets serve as a signal unit and the CoOOH nanoflakes function as a recognition element, and initially, the fluorescence of g-C3N4 is quenched by the CoOOH nanoflakes. In the absence of target, the CoOOH nanoflakes of the nanohybrid system are able to oxidize o-phenylenediamine (OPD), and the resultant oxidation product (OxOPD) quenches the blue emission of g-C3N4 and meanwhile emits orange fluorescence which acts as another signal element. However, an efficient redox reaction between ascorbic acid (AA) and CoOOH can cause decomposition of the CoOOH nanoflakes, and additionally, ALP can catalytically hydrolyze L-ascorbic acid-2-phosphate (AAP) to generate AA. Thus, in the presence of target, the CoOOH nanoflakes are destroyed by AA preferentially and the OPD is rarely oxidized to OxOPD, accompanied with strong blue emission of g-C3N4 and weak orange fluorescence from OxOPD. Target-dependent dual-signal change made the ratiometric assay possible, and also AA-induced signal variation was investigated and attributed to the stronger reducing capacity of AA than OPD. The ratiometric sensing platform for ALP activity assay provides a new perspective for the applications of two-dimensional nanomaterials to develop novel and sensitive biosensors.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ratiometric
Fluorescence
g-C3N4 nanosheets
Cobalt oxyhydroxide
Alkaline phosphatase
o-Phenylenediamine
Ascorbic acid

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst orcid primabst ref specialabst vitae

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Procedures for ratiometric response to ascorbic acid
Procedures for ratiometric detection of alkaline phosphatase activity
Characterization of CoOOH nanoflakes and g-C3N4 nanosheets
Ratiometric response to ascorbic acid and mechanism investigation
Ratiometric fluorescence detection of alkaline phosphatase activity
Analytical performance for ALP detection in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic depiction of ratiometric fluorescent analysis of alkaline phosphatase activity based on g-C3N4/CoOOH nanohybrid.

(A) TEM and high resolution TEM (inset) images of CoOOH nanoflakes. (B) XRD spectrum, (C) FT-IR spectrum, and (D) XPS analysis of CoOOH nanoflakes. (E) Co 2p region of XPS analysis for the CoOOH nanoflakes.

(A) TEM image of g-C3N4 nanosheets (inset is an amplification image). (B) XPS analysis of g-C3N4 nanosheets. (C) C 1 s spectrum and (D) N 1 s spectrum of XPS analysis for the g-C3N4 nanosheets. (E) Excitation and emission spectra of g-C3N4 nanosheets and inset is a photograph of the g-C3N4 nanosheet solution under 365 nm UV light. (F) Effect of CoOOH nanoflake concentration on the fluorescence of g-C3N4/CoOOH nanohybrid (g-C3N4 nanosheets, 70 μg/mL; CoOOH nanoflakes, from top to bottom: 0, 10, 20, 40, 80, 120, 160 μg/mL).

(A) Fluorescence spectra (excitation: 380 nm) of g-C3N4/CoOOH nanohybrid (100 μL/mL) in the presence (a) and absence (b) of AA (50 μM), and insets are photographs under 365 nm UV light. (B) UV–vis absorption spectra of g-C3N4/CoOOH (a), g-C3N4/CoOOH + OPD (b), and g-C3N4/CoOOH + OPD + AA (c). Concentrations: g-C3N4/CoOOH nanohybrid, 100 μL/mL; AA, 100 μM; OPD, 500 μM. Cyclic voltammograms of OPD (C), AA (D), and CoOOH nanoflakes (E). (F) RRS spectra of CoOOH nanoflakes (40 μg/mL) in the presence of different concentrations of OPD (from top to bottom: 0, 5, 10, 20, 50, 100, 200 μM).

(A) Fluorescence spectra against various concentrations of AA (0, 0.1, 5, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60 μM) with excitation of 380 nm. (B) Linear relationships of F 428 versus the AA concentration over the range from 0.1 to 100 μM and F 556 versus the AA concentration over the range from 0.1 to 50 μM. (C) A linear relationship of F 428/F 556 against the AA concentration over the range from 0.1 to 20 μM. Sensing system: g-C3N4/CoOOH nanohybrid, 100 μL/mL; OPD, 500 μM; PBS buffer, pH 5.5.

(A) Scheme for the AAP hydrolysis reaction by ALP catalysis to yield AA. (B) Fluorescence spectra in the presence of various concentrations of ALP (0, 1, 10, 20, 40, 50, 70, 90, 100, 150, 200 U/L) with excitation of 380 nm. (C) Linear relationships of F 428 and F 556 versus the ALP concentration in the range of 1–100 U/L, respectively. (D) A linear relationship of F 428/F 556 versus the ALP concentration in the range of 1–30 U/L. (E) Selectivity of the sensing platform for ALP detection. Concentration: ALP (50 U/L), GOx (0.5 μg/mL), HRP (0.5 μg/mL), BSA (0.5 μg/mL), and 0.1 mM for Glu, Sac, Mal, Ca2+, Mg2+, Na+, and K+. Sensing system: g-C3N4/CoOOH nanohybrid, 100 μL/mL; OPD, 500 μM.

head.ce:title: A ratiometric fluorescent strategy for alkaline phosphatase activity assay based on g-C3N4/CoOOH nanohybrid via target-triggered competitive redox reaction

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Eco-Environments in Three Gorges Reservoir Region (Ministry of Education), School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Eco-Environments in Three Gorges Reservoir Region (Ministry of Education)
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The development of ratiometric fluorescent strategy is very significant and challenging in bioanalysis. Herein, graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) nanosheets and cobalt oxyhydroxide (CoOOH) nanoflakes are exploited for ratiometric fluorescence assay of alkaline phosphatase (ALP) activity. In the g-C3N4/CoOOH nanohybrid, g-C3N4 nanosheets serve as a signal unit and the CoOOH nanoflakes function as a recognition element, and initially, the fluorescence of g-C3N4 is quenched by the CoOOH nanoflakes. In the absence of target, the CoOOH nanoflakes of the nanohybrid system are able to oxidize o-phenylenediamine (OPD), and the resultant oxidation product (OxOPD) quenches the blue emission of g-C3N4 and meanwhile emits orange fluorescence which acts as another signal element. However, an efficient redox reaction between ascorbic acid (AA) and CoOOH can cause decomposition of the CoOOH nanoflakes, and additionally, ALP can catalytically hydrolyze L-ascorbic acid-2-phosphate (AAP) to generate AA. Thus, in the presence of target, the CoOOH nanoflakes are destroyed by AA preferentially and the OPD is rarely oxidized to OxOPD, accompanied with strong blue emission of g-C3N4 and weak orange fluorescence from OxOPD. Target-dependent dual-signal change made the ratiometric assay possible, and also AA-induced signal variation was investigated and attributed to the stronger reducing capacity of AA than OPD. The ratiometric sensing platform for ALP activity assay provides a new perspective for the applications of two-dimensional nanomaterials to develop novel and sensitive biosensors.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) nanosheets, possessing graphene-like two-dimensional (2D) structure and semiconductor properties, are a new kind of fluorescent material [1,2]. Due to high condensation of tris-triazine units, g-C3N4 nanosheets can emit strong blue fluorescence. Endowed with good biocompatibility as well as large surface areas, g-C3N4 nanosheets make it possible to develop useful and capable fluorescent sensing platforms for bioanalysis and bioimaging applications [3,4]. Up to now, several fluorescent biosensors for metal ions, biothiols, tyrosinase activity, and glucose based on g-C3N4 nanosheets have been developed successfully employing various mechanisms [5–8].

Cobalt oxyhydroxide (CoOOH) nanoflakes, a type of emerging 2D nanomaterial, have attracted much research interest and been proposed for numerous applications including photocarrier, supercapacitor, catalysis, biosensing and bioimaging [9–12]. CoOOH nanoflakes have abundant hydroxyl groups on the surface and good water dispersibility. The development of CoOOH nanoflakes-based sensors is in an infant stage, and most of the sensors are established mainly by using the sensitive quenching capacity to fluorescence, such as DNA sensing [13], ascorbic acid (AA) sensing and bioimaging [14,15], α-glucosidase inhibitor screening [16], polynucleotide kinase detection [17], and pyrophosphate anion assay [18]. In addition, as enzyme mimics, CoOOH nanoflakes were exploited to catalytically oxidize 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) for analytical applications recently [19,20]. The utility of CoOOH nanoflakes to create novel and versatile sensors is worth pursuing.

Alkaline phosphatase (ALP) functions as the physiological role for catalyzing dephosphorylation processes in organisms [21]. As a very important enzyme, ALP dwells in many tissues of organisms, such as bone, liver, intestine, kidney, and placenta. The biological function of ALP is not identical in different tissues through dephosphorylating different substrates like DNA and proteins. Therefore, the abnormal ALP level is closely related to many diseases, including bone diseases, diabetes, liver dysfunction and cancer, and prostatic cancer [22–24]. ALP thereby serves as an important biomarker for disease diagnosis usually. The creation of reliable and convenient method for sensitively analyzing ALP is very significant and desirable for both biomedical research and clinical diagnoses. In recent years, various fluorescent sensors of ALP have been constructed based on various photoluminescent materials, including carbon dots [25,26], semiconductor quantum dots [27], noble metal nanoclusters [28,29], conjugated polymers [30], and so on [31–33]. However, these sensors employ the“turn-on” or “turn-off” single fluorescence as the read-out signal and thus, may be easily affected by interferences which could come from some analyte-independent factors, such as sample background signal, environmental changes, and the fluctuation of detection conditions [34,35]. In contrast, ratiometric assay by recording two or more response signals can avoid most of these interferences to some extent. To date, there has been few reports about ratiometric sensing of ALP activity. For instance, Deng et al. proposed a ratiometric fluorescent system for ALP activity assay by using infinite coordination polymer nanoparticles [30]; Zheng et al. developed a ratiometric fluorescent probe of ALP using betaine-modified polyethylenimine based on an excimer/monomer conversion principle [36]; Song et al. synthesized a water-soluble phosphorylated chalcone derivative and exploited it as a ratiometric fluorescent probe for ALP visual detection on the basis of aggregation-induced emission processes [37]. However, it is not desired for most of these ratiometric fluorescent probes that they need complex and time-consuming organic synthesis process. Thus, the development of new ratiometric fluorescent strategies with easy preparation and good performance for ALP is very desirable.

Herein, combined two 2D nanomaterials, g-C3N4 nanosheets and CoOOH nanoflakes, are exploited for ratiometric fluorescent analysis of ALP activity. Scheme 1 describes the principle of ratiometric assay of ALP activity based on the g-C3N4/CoOOH nanohybrid via target-triggered competitive redox reactions. In the g-C3N4/CoOOH nanohybrid solution, o-phenylenediamine (OPD) can be oxidized and the resulting oxidation product (OxOPD) has an orange fluorescence; meanwhile, the blue emission of g-C3N4 is quenched by the OxOPD. However, AA can cause break-up of the CoOOH nanoflakes because of an efficient redox reaction between AA and CoOOH. Thus, in the presence of AA, the CoOOH nanoflakes are destroyed by AA preferentially, and the OPD is rarely oxidized to OxOPD. As a consequence, the strong blue emission of g-C3N4 and weak orange fluorescence from OxOPD can be obtained. AA-induced dual-signal change makes ratiometric assay possible. Additionally, ALP can catalytically hydrolyze L-ascorbic acid-2-phosphate (AAP) to produce AA. Therefore, the ratiometric fluorescence detection of ALP activity can be achieved with the aid of AAP. The proposed method provides not only a sensitive and simple detection of ALP activity but also a novel perspective for the applications of 2D nanomaterials to develop sensitive biosensors.

Ascorbic acid (AA), glucose (Glu), saccharose (Sac), maltose (Mal), melamine, and bovine serum albumin (BSA) were supplied by Aladdin Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Horseradish peroxidase (HRP), alkaline phosphatase (ALP), and glucose oxidase (GOx) were bought from Shanghai Sangon Biotech Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). o-Phenylenediamine (OPD) and L-ascorbic acid-2-phosphate (AAP) were purchased from Chengdu Kong Chemical Reagents Factory (Chengdu, China) and Yuanye Biotech Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China), respectively. All other chemicals were of analytical reagent grade and used as obtained. Human serum samples were supplied by Southwest University Hospital (Chongqing, China). High-purity water with a resistivity of 18.2 MΩ cm was used in this study.

Fluorescence spectra were obtained using an F-2700 spectrofluorometer (Hitachi, Japan) with the slits (excitation/emission) of 10/10 nm and the photomultiplier tube (PMT) voltage of 400 V. The resonance Rayleigh scattering (RRS) spectra were also recorded by using the F-2700 spectrofluorometer. UV–vis absorption measurements were performed on a UV–vis 2450 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). A transmission electron microscope (JEM 1200EX, JEOL, Japan) was used to obtain transmission electron microscopic (TEM) images. A Dimension Icon10800 atomic force microscope (Bruker, Germany) was utilized to obtain atomic force microscopic (AFM) images. An Escalab 250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) was used for X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) characterizations. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy analysis was conducted on a Bruker IFS 113v spectrometer (Bruker, Germany). Cyclic voltammetry experiments were taken on a CHI 660D electrochemical workstation (Shanghai CHI Instruments Co., China). A pH meter (FE28, Mettler-Toledo, Switzerland) was used to determine pH values.

Based on a reported procedure [12], the CoOOH nanoflakes were obtained by the method with minor modification. Briefly, a portion of cobalt chloride (CoCl2) solution (50 mL, 10 mM) was mixed with NaOH solution (1.5 mL, 1.0 M). The mixture was treated by ultrasonic for 5 min and then centrifuged at 4000 rpm (20 min). After that, the precipitate was re-dispersed into water (50 mL) and a portion of NaClO (2.5 mL, 0.9 M) was added, followed by ultrasonic treatment for 20 min. Then, the mixture was centrifuged (12,000 rpm, 10 min) and after washed three times with water, the precipitate was finally re-dispersed into 50 mL of ultrapure water, which was the CoOOH nanoflake stock solution.

At first, the bulk g-C3N4 was synthesized by heating melamine according to a literature method [1]. Briefly, an amount of melamine (4.0 g) was heated at 550 °C for 2 h under air condition to obtain bulk g-C3N4 powder. Next, the g-C3N4 nanosheets was prepared by liquid exfoliating of the bulk g-C3N4 in water [1,5]. Typically, a portion of bulk g-C3N4 powder (0.1 g) was dispersed into ultrapure water (50 mL) and then treated by ultrasonic for about 20 h. Subsequently, the obtained suspension was centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 30 min to get rid of the unexfoliated bulk g-C3N4 and large-area nanosheets, and the supernatant was collected as the g-C3N4 nanosheet stock solution.

The g-C3N4/CoOOH nanohybrid was obtained through mixing a certain amount of g-C3N4 nanosheets and CoOOH nanoflakes. In brief, 1 mL of CoOOH nanoflake solution (4 mg/mL) was added to g-C3N4 nanosheet solution (9 mL, 70 μg/mL), and the mixture was mixed by oscillation and then incubated for 5 min under ambient condition. After that, the g-C3N4/CoOOH nanohybrid was prepared successfully.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedures for ratiometric response to ascorbic acid
ce:section.ce:para: At first, a volume of g-C3N4/CoOOH nanohybrid solution (100 μL) and a portion of AA solution (100 μL) with different concentrations were mixed with 750 μL of PBS buffer (1/15 M, pH 5.5). The OPD solution (50 μL, 10 mM) was then introduced to the mixtures. Subsequently, the mixed solutions were kept in a water bath at 60 °C for 1 h. Finally, the fluorescence emission spectra were collected at an excitation wavelength of 380 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedures for ratiometric detection of alkaline phosphatase activity
ce:section.ce:para: For sensitively ratiometric fluorescence analysis of ALP activity, a portion of 100 μL of ALP solution with different concentrations and 20 μL of AAP (10 mM) were firstly mixed with 80 μL of PBS buffer (1/15 M, pH 9.1). The mixtures were incubated for 30 min in a water bath at 37 °C. Then, 100 μL of g-C3N4/CoOOH nanohybrid, 50 μL of OPD (10 mM), and 550 μL of PBS buffer (1/15 M, pH 5.5) were introduced into the mixture, followed by further incubation in a water bath at 60 °C for 1 h. At last, the fluorescence emission spectra were obtained by the excitation wavelength of 380 nm. The detection of ALP in real samples was similar to the ALP analysis mentioned above except that a 10-fold diluted serum sample was used to replace the ALP solution.

For cyclic voltammetric experiments, the glassy carbon electrodes (GCE) were polished to a smooth surface with 0.05 μm alumina powder for 10 min, and then washed by ultrapure water and dried in a nitrogen stream. For AA and OPD measurements, AA or OPD was added to pH 5.5 PBS buffer, and the cyclic voltammogram was obtained at a sweeping rate of 50 mV/s ranging from −1.0 to 1.0 V versus Ag/AgCl. For CoOOH nanoflake test, a portion of the CoOOH nanoflake solution (5 μL, 0.4 mg/mL) was dropwise added on the pretreated GCE using a pipette, and then maintained in a vacuum at 25 °C for 2 h. After that, the cyclic voltammogram was recorded at a sweeping rate of 50 mV/s ranging from 0.2 to −1.2 V versus Ag/AgCl in PBS buffer (pH 5.5).

The RRS spectra were obtained using the spectrofluorometer with the slits (excitation/emission) of 5/5 nm and the PMT voltage of 400 V by synchronous scanning at λem = λex (i.e., Δλ = 0 nm) in the wavelength range of 250–650 nm. For RRS measurements of CoOOH nanoflakes, a portion of 10 μL of the CoOOH nanoflake (4 mg/mL) solution and 100 μL of OPD solution with different levels were added to 890 μL of pH 5.5 PBS buffer, followed by shaking well. Then, the mixtures were kept in a water bath at 60 °C for 1 h, and finally, the RRS spectra were collected on the spectrofluorometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of CoOOH nanoflakes and g-C3N4 nanosheets
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1 A is a TEM image which reveals that the CoOOH nanoflakes own layered structure, and the high resolution TEM image shows their clear lattice (inset of Fig. 1A). Corresponding AFM characterization of the CoOOH nanoflakes displays that the thickness is less than 2.0 nm (Fig. S1 A and S1B). The powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurement reveals that the crystalline CoOOH nanoflakes have an obvious diffraction peak located at 2θ = 20.2° (Fig. 1B). As depicted in Fig. 1C, the FT-IR spectroscopy analysis shows that the CoOOH nanoflakes have characteristic absorption bands located at 3386 cm−1 (−OH), 1628 cm−1 (Co − O), and 578 cm−1 (Co − O2−), which are consistent with previous reports [19]. The XPS analysis was further performed, as exhibited in Fig. 1D and 1E, and two peaks at 795.3 eV (Co 2p1/2) and 780.2 eV (Co 2p3/2) suggest that cobalt of the CoOOH nanoflakes is Co(III) oxidation state [12]. Moreover, the UV–vis absorption spectrum displays two absorption bands centered at 247 and 398 nm for the CoOOH nanoflakes (Fig. S2). The CoOOH nanoflake solution shows a brown color under visible light (inset a of Fig. S2) and a Tyndall phenomenon can be clearly observed (inset b of Fig. S2). These results adequately proved the successful preparation of the CoOOH nanoflakes.

The g-C3N4 nanosheets were prepared in accordance with a published method [1], and characterized in detail to ensure successful preparation. The TEM image (Fig. 2 A) shows that the g-C3N4 nanosheets have a sheet structure, which was further verified by the AFM measurement (Fig. S3). The XPS characterization reveals that g-C3N4 nanosheets contain three main elements (C, N, O) (Fig. 2B). The existence of oxygen may be derived from some oxygen-containing groups produced in the heating and liquid exfoliation processes. The ratio of N/C of the g-C3N4 nanosheets is about 1.37, which is close to that of the stoichiometric ratio (about 1.33) of g-C3N4. As shown in Fig. 2C, the C 1 s spectrum can be divided into three peaks which are separately located at 284.1, 285.8, and 287.4 eV, corresponding to graphitic carbon, the carbon of sp3 hybridization (COH), and the carbon of sp2 hybridization (NCN), respectively. Fig. 2D shows the N 1 s spectrum and it can be fitted to three peaks, centered at 397.8, 398.6, and 400.0 eV, which are attributed to the aromatic nitrogen (CNC) of sp2 hybridization, tertiary nitrogen of (N−(C)3) or HN−(C)2, and quaternary N in the aromatic groups, respectively. The weak peak at 403.6 eV on the N 1 s spectrum is caused by charging effects or localization of positive charge in heterocycles [6]. Fig. S4 shows the FT-IR spectrum of the g-C3N4 nanosheets. The absorption peak at 808 cm−1 is consistent with the characteristic vibration of triazine ring structure. The absorption bands originating from stretching vibration of CN and CN can be found in the region of 1000 to 1800 cm−1. The broad absorption band from 3000 to 3600 cm−1 belongs to NH and OH vibrations and hydrogen-bonding interactions.

The optical properties of the g-C3N4 nanosheets were next investigated. As presented in Fig. S5, an absorption peak at 321 nm in the UV − vis absorption spectrum (above 250 nm) can be found. The g-C3N4 solution exhibits brightly blue emission under UV light of 365 nm, and it can be seen from Fig. 2E that the maximum excitation and emission wavelengths are 320 and 440 nm, respectively. The emission peak has no obvious shift with different excitation lights from 300 to 390 nm (Fig. S6). Previous studies have demonstrated that CoOOH nanoflakes are a kind of favorable fluorescence quencher and can quench fluorescence of various materials (such as persistent luminescence nanoparticles, graphene quantum dots, carbon dots, etc.) via fluorescence resonance energy transfer [14–16]. The possibility of energy transfer between CoOOH nanoflakes and g-C3N4 was investigated. Fig. S7 reveals that the absorption spectrum of CoOOH nanoflakes is heavily overlapped with the emission spectrum of g-C3N4, which promises the generation of energy transfer. As presented in Fig. 2F, the fluorescence of the g-C3N4/CoOOH nanohybrids is decreased progressively with raising the amount of CoOOH nanoflakes, proving the good fluorescence quenching capacity of the CoOOH nanoflakes to g-C3N4.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric response to ascorbic acid and mechanism investigation
ce:section.ce:para: As presented in Fig. 3 A, the fluorescence at 428 nm (F 428) of the g-C3N4/CoOOH nanohybrid containing OPD is weak and the fluorescence at 556 nm (F 556) is strong in the absence of AA. However, with the introduction of AA, the F 428 of the system increases remarkably whereas the F 556 declines. The variation of the two signals indicates that ratiometric fluorescent sensing of AA is possible.

CoOOH nanoflakes have relatively strong oxidizing ability because of the existence of Co3+. Previous researches have fully demonstrated that an efficient redox reaction can take place between AA and CoOOH, causing that CoOOH is reduced to Co2+ ion [14–16]. Thus, it would result in break-up of CoOOH nanoflakes. The change of absorption spectrum of the CoOOH nanoflakes with the addition of AA demonstrates the reduction and decomposition (Fig. S8). OPD, a typical chromogenic substrate, can be catalytically oxidized to produce OxOPD (2,3-diaminophenazine is the main product generally) by the horseradish peroxidase/H2O2 system [8,38]. The resultant OxOPD solution displays a yellow color with an UV–vis absorption band around 420 nm and emits bright orange fluorescence. Firstly, the critical role of CoOOH nanoflakes to the oxidation of OPD was investigated. As displayed in Fig. S9A and S9B, under the same reaction conditions, compared to sole OPD, the mixture of OPD and CoOOH nanoflakes has an intensive absorption at 420 nm as well as a remarkable fluorescence emission at 556 nm, which is similar to those of the reaction product of peroxidase/H2O2 and OPD [8,38]. The results suggest the oxidation of OPD. However, in the presence of AA, the OPD was rarely oxidized (curve d in Fig. S9A and S9B). The g-C3N4/CoOOH hybrid containing OPD has similar optical characteristic at absorption band of 420 nm compared to the reaction mixture of OPD and CoOOH nanoflakes (Fig. 3B). In addition, in order to verify the CoOOH nanoflake function in the process of OPD oxidation, we also measured whether Co2+ ions have a similar capacity. As exhibited in Fig. S9A and S9B (curve b), no strong absorption band at 420 nm and fluorescence emission at 556 nm are found for the mixture of Co2+ ions and OPD.

Some published literature attributed the chromogenic reaction of some substrates like TMB in the presence of MnO2 and CoOOH nanosheets owning oxidizing ability to their oxidase-like catalyzing activity [20,39]. In this work, to explore the real role of CoOOH nanoflakes in the oxidation process of OPD, some relevant experiments were carried out. At first, we performed the cyclic voltammetric experiments using a conventional three-electrode system, in which electrochemical redox behavior of AA, OPD, and CoOOH nanoflakes were investigated. Fig. 3C–E shows the cyclic voltammograms (CV) of OPD, AA, and CoOOH nanoflakes, respectively (a sweeping rate of 50 mV/s ranging from −1.0 to 1.0 V for OPD and AA and ranging from 0.2 to −1.2 V for CoOOH nanoflakes). It can be clearly seen that the anodic peak potentials (Ep,a) of OPD and AA are 0.376 and 0.171 V, respectively, and the cathodic peak potential (Ep,c) of CoOOH nanoflakes is -0.622 V. The results imply that both AA and OPD can be oxidized by the CoOOH nanoflakes, but the reducing capacity of AA may be stronger than that of OPD under same conditions. To further explore whether the CoOOH nanoflakes are reduced and decomposed by OPD, the RRS spectra were recorded for the CoOOH nanoflakes reacting with various concentrations OPD. As displayed in Fig. 3F, the RRS intensity is decreased gradually with increasing OPD concentration. According to scattering theory, the intensity of RRS is closely related to the size and concentration of scatterers [40,41]. The decline of RRS intensity demonstrated the decomposition of CoOOH nanoflakes. In addition, the CoOOH nanoflakes in the presence of high level of OPD are not found by a TEM, further revealing the decomposition of CoOOH nanoflakes. Overall, the results illustrate that the CoOOH nanoflakes serve as an oxidant rather than only a catalyst in the OPD oxidation process.

On the basis of above discussion, the dual-signal response principle can be concluded as follows. Initially, the blue fluorescence of g-C3N4 nanosheets is quenched by the CoOOH nanoflakes in the g-C3N4/CoOOH nanosystem. In the absence of target of AA, the OPD is oxidized to generate OxOPD with orange fluorescence, and meanwhile, the blue emission of g-C3N4 is still quenched by the OxOPD via electron and energy transfer [8], though CoOOH nanoflakes are decomposed. Namely, without AA, the sensing platform emits orange fluorescence and meanwhile blue fluorescence is suppressed. However, in the presence of AA, the CoOOH nanoflakes are reduced and destroyed by AA preferentially, and thus the OPD is rarely oxidized to OxOPD. Therefore, the strong blue emission from g-C3N4 and weak orange fluorescence from OxOPD would be obtained. The changes of two fluorescence make ratiometric assay possible.

The fluorescence response to AA was due to the reducing power of AA, and thereby we also investigated the reducing capacity of five other biological reducing molecules to CoOOH nanoflakes, including glutathione, cysteine, adrenaline, uric acid, and dopamine. As presented in Fig. S10, cysteine, adrenaline, and glutathione can also induce fluorescence decline of CoOOH/OPD system, but their ability to decrease fluorescence is not comparable to that of AA, indicating that AA owns good superiority to reduce CoOOH to Co2+ ions. Next, the sensitivity of the g-C3N4/CoOOH ratiometric sensing system for AA was evaluated. To obtain efficient detection, the reaction conditions for OPD oxidation were optimized firstly (Fig. S11), and the optimal conditions were as follows: reaction time: 1 h; pH value: 5.5 (PBS buffer). As depicted in Fig. 4 A, the fluorescence centered at 556 nm (F 556) of the g-C3N4/CoOOH nanosystem decreases progressively with raising AA concentration, whereas a gradual increase for blue emission (F 428) is found. Fig. 4B displays the plot of fluorescence intensity changes of F 556 and F 428 with increasing AA concentration. Linear relationships for F 428 (R 2 = 0.998) in the range of 0.1–100 μM and for F 556 (R 2 = 0. 991) in the range of 0.1–50 μM can be found. The ratio fluorescence intensity (F 428/F 556) has a good linear relationship (R 2 = 0.992) against AA over the range of 0.1–20 μM (Fig. 4C). The detection limit for AA was estimated as 0.098 μM according to 3σ/slope (σ represents the standard deviation of the blank signals). The results demonstrate the sensitive response of the analysis system to AA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric fluorescence detection of alkaline phosphatase activity
ce:section.ce:para: The ratiometric and sensitive response of the assay platform to AA enables us to develop a ratiometric analytical system for probing ALP activity using the g-C3N4/CoOOH nanohybrid because ALP can catalytically hydrolyze AAP to produce AA (Fig. 5 A). Fig. S12 A shows that the fluorescence at 428 nm (F 428) of the reaction mixture of g-C3N4/CoOOH hybrid containing OPD and AAP is very weak and the fluorescence at 556 nm (F 556) is strong without ALP. In contrast, with the introduction of ALP, the F 428 of the system increases whereas the F 556 declines obviously. Corresponding UV–vis absorption spectra also proved the occurrence of the hydrolysis reaction in the presence of ALP (Fig. S12B). The results imply the possibility of ALP ratiometric assay with the assist of AAP based on the g-C3N4/CoOOH nanohybrid.

After optimizing the reaction time and temperature of ALP catalysis (optimal conditions: 37 °C, 30 min; Fig. S13), the sensitivity was investigated. As exhibited in Fig. 5B, the fluorescence response is sensitive to ALP, and F 556 declines and F 428 increases gradually with raising the level of ALP. Fig. 5C exhibits the plot of fluorescence intensity changes of F 428 and F 556 with increasing ALP concentration. Linear fitting for F 428 (R 2 = 0.990) over the concentration range of 1–100 U/L and for F 556 (R 2 = 0.991) over the concentration range of 1–100 U/L were obtained. A good linear relationship (R 2 = 0.991) was found between the ratio fluorescence intensity (F 428/F 556) and the ALP concentration in the range of 1–30 U/L (Fig. 5D). The detection limit for ALP was estimated to be 0.92 U/L according to 3σ/slope (σ denotes the standard deviation of blank signals). We made a comparison about linear range and detection limit between our method and previously reported sensors for ALP analysis, as presented in Table S1. Satisfactorily, it can be seen that our proposed ratiometric analysis system is comparable to most of those methods. Besides, it is known that the ratiometric detection, which can provide a self-calibration, has better anti-interference ability and can make assay results more accurate [34,35].

The capacity of good anti-interference for a sensor is desirable in practical applications. As a result, the selectivity of the ratiometric assay platform was assessed for ALP detection. Some possible interferents including GOx, BSA, HRP, Glu, Sac, Mal, Ca2+, Mg2+, K+, and Na+, were introduced to the detection system. As revealed in Fig. 5E and F, the addition of other components to the analysis system is not capable of causing a remarkable signal variation, and only introducing ALP can cause an apparent fluorescence change for the detection system. Accordingly, owing to the specificity of enzyme catalysis, the established ratiometric sensing platform shows a good selectivity for ALP detection. However, it must be pointed out that the existence of reducing agents such as cysteine and AA in samples would certainly interfere to some extent. Thus, if analytic samples contain a high concentration of reducing agents like AA and cysteine, a pretreatment is needed, such as centrifugation against an ultrafilter to remove the reducing small molecules.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance for ALP detection in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The applications in real sample assay for a developed biosensor are challenging owing to possible interferences in the complex matrix. In order to assess the practical applications of the ratiometric method on the basis of g-C3N4/CoOOH nanohybrid, the ALP levels of human serum samples were measured and also the experiments of recovery were carried out through the analysis of ALP-spiked human serums. Generally, the amount of reducing species like AA in fasting serum is not at a high level and so the diluted human samples were tested directly. The corresponding assay results are listed in Table S2. The recoveries of ALP assay over the range of 105.3–109.0% and the relative standard deviations (RSDs) over the range of 2.56–6.56% were obtained. The results demonstrate good repeatability and reliability of the ratiometric method, indicating a potential capacity for ALP detection in practical applications.

In conclusion, we describe that the g-C3N4/CoOOH nanohybrid system has been exploited for ratiometric fluorescence assay of ALP activity. In the nanohybrid system, the g-C3N4 nanosheets serve as a signal unit while the CoOOH nanoflakes function as a recognition element. The CoOOH nanoflakes have relatively strong oxidizing ability. In the absence of target, the CoOOH nanoflakes of the nanohybrid system oxidized OPD to produce OxOPD which quenched the blue emission of g-C3N4 and emitted orange fluorescence at the same time. However, an efficient redox reaction between AA and CoOOH can cause break-up of the CoOOH nanoflakes, and furthermore ALP can catalytically hydrolyze AAP to produce AA. Consequently, in the presence of ALP, the CoOOH nanoflakes were reduced and destroyed by AA preferentially, but the OPD was rarely oxidized, accompanied with strong blue emission of g-C3N4 and weak orange fluorescence from OxOPD. Thus, ratiometric analysis of ALP activity has been achieved based on the AA-dependent dual-signal response. In addition, the mechanism of AA-induced signal variation was explored and attributed to stronger reducing capacity of AA than OPD, and the analysis by CV, RRS, and TEM revealed that the CoOOH nanoflakes served as an oxidant rather than only a catalyst in the OPD oxidation process. In addition, the ratiometric sensing platform has been utilized to ALP detection in human serum samples and good analysis results have been obtained. The proposed ratiometric analytical system based on g-C3N4/CoOOH nanohybrid offers a new perspective for the application of 2D nanomaterials to design novel and sensitive biosensors.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors deeply acknowledge financial support for this work from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21675131), the Natural Science Foundation of Chongqing (No. CSTC-2015jcyjB50001), and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (XDJK2018D012).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2018.12.052.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Shi Gang Liu is a doctoral student in School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, China. His research interests are the developments of chemical sensors and biosensors.
Lei Han is a doctoral student in School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, China. His major research interests include photochemistry and spectrum analysis.
Na Li is a doctoral student in School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, China. Her major research interest is spectrum analysis.
Yu Zhu Fan is a doctoral student in School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, China. Her major research interest is the application of fluorescent sensors.

Yu Zhu Yang is a doctoral student in School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, China. Her research interests focus mainly on the synthesis of fluorescent nanomaterials and their chemosensors application.

Nian Bing Li is a full professor of chemistry at Southwest University, China. He received his MS degree in physical chemistry in 1997 and PhD degree in material science in 2000 from Chongqing University. During 2000–2002, he was a postdoctoral research fellow in Fuzhou University, China. Since 2006–2007, he was a postdoctoral research fellow in Korea Advanced Institute of Science and Technology (KAIST), Korea. His research interests are focused on size controlled synthesis, characterization, and size-dependent property study of metal nanocusters, nanobiosensing, and electrochemisty.

Hong Qun Luo is a full professor of chemistry at Southwest University, China. She received her MS degree in environmental chemistry from Sichuan University in 1991 and PhD degree in analytical chemistry from Southwest China Normal University in 2002. During 2006–2007, she was a visiting scholar in Tohoku University, Japan. Her research is focused on molecular spectroscopy and electrochemical sensor.
